title: Error reading image ... image not known
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

Several times since I switched to podman from docker I end up getting an `image not known` error. I'm unable to remove the offending image and the only solution I've found (besides manually combing through the storage and json files to clean it out) is to wipe ~/.local/share/containers/storage and start over.

I typically first notice the error when running `podman image`:

```
$ podman image ls
ERRO[0003] error checking if image is a parent "7030953edf669a9fcdd40594d588ea7a6af389c65f6231ee1126066267605963": error reading image "f0c5b4e82e11c970a2f38198b2eaeacd6479b6bc12b90960887cf456ad83ae21": image not known
```

I can't remove the offending image:

```
$ podman image rm f0c5b4e82e11c970a2f38198b2eaeacd6479b6bc12b90960887cf456ad83ae21
Error: error reading image "f0c5b4e82e11c970a2f38198b2eaeacd6479b6bc12b90960887cf456ad83ae21": image not known

$ podman image rm -f f0c5b4e82e11c970a2f38198b2eaeacd6479b6bc12b90960887cf456ad83ae21
Error: error reading image "f0c5b4e82e11c970a2f38198b2eaeacd6479b6bc12b90960887cf456ad83ae21": image not known
```

I can't do a prune either:

```
$ podman image prune -a
Error: failed to prune image: Image used by f2061acd5a510ad39a7ec7923d2a1aa416210ef4c2cd5c28afec92d6c4a677a1: image is in use by a container
```

Where does `f2061acd5a510ad39a7ec7923d2a1aa416210ef4c2cd5c28afec92d6c4a677a1` come from? It's not in the list of images or containers, and I can't remove it either.

comments.body: `f2061acd5a510ad39a7ec7923d2a1aa416210ef4c2cd5c28afec92d6c4a677a1` looks like a container ID - probably a failure to remove the underlying storage of a container. You may want to try a `podman rm --storage f2061acd5a510ad39a7ec7923d2a1aa416210ef4c2cd5c28afec92d6c4a677a1`

It looks like that changes the error I get when I try to prune, but I'm still getting `image not known`:

```
$ podman rm --storage f2061acd5a510ad39a7ec7923d2a1aa416210ef4c2cd5c28afec92d6c4a677a1
f2061acd5a510ad39a7ec7923d2a1aa416210ef4c2cd5c28afec92d6c4a677a1
```

> probably a failure to remove the underlying storage of a container

I do use `podman run --rm` a lot. Is it possible that podman isn't always able to remove the container if files have been written inside it that my user doesn't have permissions to delete? I'm not sure if that makes sense; this isn't my area of expertise.
Hm. Do you have any Buildah containers created? This looks like it may have something to do with lingering Buildah containers after builds.
A `buildah containers --all` might be enlightening
I don't suspect this is `--rm`. We're possibly seeing either Buildah containers, or containers that saw errors during removal.

```
$ buildah containers --all
```

That's probably why `podman prune` is failing - we have a large quantity of images which Podman thinks it can prune, as Podman isn't using them, but they are in use by Buildah.

We should probably just ignore those errors - if an image fails to prune because it's in use by a container, we should not have been pruning them in the first place.
This doesn't resolve the `ERRO[0003] error checking if image is a parent` situation, though, which seems separate
Should I create a separate issue for `podman prune`?
Agree - it'll probably be a trivial fix, so we should track it separately.

I just created a new issue for `podman prune` here: https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/3983

Thanks!

One thing in common I see with the original report is that both his configuration and the original one use directories where systemd-tmpfiles can clean up.  We don't set the sticky bit for any of out files (and we cannot really do that for image files).

@bonzini are you using the default configuration for systemd-tmpfiles or have you tweaked it?

In the default configuration I've on Fedora 32, I see:

```
$ grep var/tmp /usr/lib/tmpfiles.d/tmp.conf 
q /var/tmp 1777 root root 30d
```
Wait, what? I was completely unaware of this. This sounds like it could break Podman's alive lock on long-running systems...
Nope, I have not tweaked the configuration in any way. That's a great catch @giuseppe!!

> Wait, what? I was completely unaware of this. This sounds like it could break Podman's alive lock on long-running systems...

yes, we should not use `/var/tmp` or `/tmp` for long lived sessions.  They are really usable only with short lived sessions (e.g. CI, builds) and not persistent data.

We could probably fix the rundir side, and set the sticky bit for each file, but that is difficult to get right as we will certainly miss some of the files.

I don't see any way in systemd for doing it for directories (unless the directory is a mountpoint named `"lost+found"` or there is a flock on the directory.  The cleanup doesn't cross mount points, so it won't mess with `$STORAGE/overlay`, at least if there is a container running; but it can get to everything else.

As a workaround, I can't even think of a good place where we could periodically `touch` the directory, 10d for `/tmp` is not that long for doing it in Podman itself.

> As a workaround, I can't even think of a good place where we could periodically touch the directory, 10d for /tmp is not that long for doing it in Podman itself.

No problem, I'll move the graphroot under /var/lib and just regenerate all the images, in effect it's just like dropping a cache. One thing that can be done from your side is to add a "podman image fsck" command that drops inaccessible images from the json directory, which would un-hose my graphroot.
Is it possible to create a libpod-specific directory in tmp, move everything there and set the sticky bit there?

> Is it possible to create a libpod-specific directory in tmp, move everything there and set the sticky bit there?

the sticky bit cannot be set for a directory for tmpfiles to skip cleaning it, because the sticky bit has already a different meaning for directories.



> One thing that can be done from your side is to add a "podman image fsck" command that drops inaccessible images from the json directory, which would un-hose my graphroot.

that can be useful in general to have, but for this particular issue I'd still feel uncomfortable that files can suddenly disappear from the storage.  If systemd-tmpfiles deletes the right whiteout file, then the super secret file covered by the image uppermost layer becomes accessible again...
@rhatdan should we worry about the security implications having the storage on such directories can have or is it out of scope/wrong configuration?

>  If systemd-tmpfiles deletes the right whiteout file, then the super secret file covered by the image uppermost layer becomes accessible again...

It's not just whiteouts, deleting random files in an image can also screw up the installation or apply the wrong configuration (e.g. it could enable password-less sudo).  In fact before noticing this symptom, I had found the top image in the chain to be completely broken because half of /usr had disappeared from the `fedora:30` base image.

So I don't think the security implications are serious; it's just a very bad idea to remove random files from the storage graphroot, no matter if you do it directly or systemd-tmpfiles decides to.

@bonzini Yes it is a bad idea to put storage on /tmp.
@giuseppe I don't think we can prevent users from sticking storage into a random directly like this.  There are potential security attacks from one user to another I guess, if we don't lock down the search paths in the containers directory.
